subject: Chemistry
q: What will be the decreasing order of basic strength of the following conjugate bases? \({ }^{-} \mathrm{OH}, \mathrm{R} \overline{\mathrm{O}}, \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CO} \overline{\mathrm{O}}, \mathrm{C} \overline{\mathrm{I}}\)
question: What will be the decreasing order of basic strength of the following conjugate bases? \({ }^{-} \mathrm{OH}, \mathrm{R} \overline{\mathrm{O}}, \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CO} \overline{\mathrm{O}}, \mathrm{C} \overline{\mathrm{I}}\)
solution:
Strong acid have weak conjugate base Acidic strength : $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{Cl}>\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH}>\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}>\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{OH}$
Conjugate base strength : $\mathrm{Cl}^{-} < \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}^{-} < \overline{\mathrm{O}} \mathrm{H} < \mathrm{RO}^{-}$

So configuration of Gd2+=[Xe]4f75d1

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following has least tendency to liberate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> from mineral acids?
question: Which of the following has least tendency to liberate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> from mineral acids?
solution:
Element M E⊝/V
Mn -1.18
Ni -0.25
Cu 0.34
Zn -0.76

As we can see that Copper is least electropositive metal among the given options. So, it does not react with dilute sulphuric acid or other mineral acids, liberating hydrogen because copper is lower in electrochemical series than hydrogen. All other metals will react with mineral acids to liberate hydrogen gas.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Among \(\mathrm{CrO}, \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3\) and \(\mathrm{CrO}_3\), the sum of spin-only magnetic moment values of basic and amphoteric oxides is _______ \(10^{-2} \mathrm{BM}\) (nearest integer).
(Given atomic number of \(\mathrm{Cr}\) is 24)
question:
Among \(\mathrm{CrO}, \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3\) and \(\mathrm{CrO}_3\), the sum of spin-only magnetic moment values of basic and amphoteric oxides is _______ \(10^{-2} \mathrm{BM}\) (nearest integer).
(Given atomic number of \(\mathrm{Cr}\) is 24)
solution:
CrO Basic oxide $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ Amphoteric oxide In $\mathrm{CrO}, \mathrm{Cr}$ exist as $\mathrm{Cr}^{+2}$ and have $\mu$ only $=4.90$ In $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3, \mathrm{Cr}$ exist as $\mathrm{Cr}^{+3}$ and have $\mu$ only $=3.87$ Sum of spin only magnetic moment $\begin{aligned} & =4.90+3.87=8.77 \\ & \mu_{\text {only }}=877 \times 10^{-2} \end{aligned}$
Ans. 877

subject: Chemistry
q: The oxidation state of manganese in the product obtained in a reaction of potassium permanganate and hydrogen peroxide in basic medium is
question: The oxidation state of manganese in the product obtained in a reaction of potassium permanganate and hydrogen peroxide in basic medium is
solution:
This KMnO4 is a powerful oxidizing agent in neutral, acidic, and alkaline medium. The H2O2 is also an oxidizing agent but in the reaction of KMnO4 with the H2O2. The H2O2 loses its electron and acts as a reducing agent. In an acidic medium, theMnO4- ions involve the addition of 5 electrons and the two peroxide ions give out two electrons. In dilute alkaline medium or in neutral medium MnO4- ions involve the addition of 3 electrons. The reaction in basic medium is given below.

2KMnO4+3H2O2⟶basic medium2MnO2+4+3O2+2H2O+2KOH

The oxidation state of Mn in MnO2 is +4.

subject: Chemistry
q: The spin-only magnetic moment value of the most basic oxide of vanadium among <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math>' <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>5</mn></msub></math> is____B.M. (Nearest integer)
question: The spin-only magnetic moment value of the most basic oxide of vanadium among <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math>' <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>5</mn></msub></math> is____B.M. (Nearest integer)
solution:
V2O3 is the most basic oxide among the given oxides.

In V2O3, Vanadium is in +3 oxidation state

V3+⇒Ar3d24s0

So, 2 unpaired electrons are there,

Its magnetic moment is equal to μ=nn+2 BM=22+2 BM

=2.83 BM ≃ 3 BM

(n= number of unpaired electrons)

subject: Chemistry
q: The total number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Mn</mi><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math> bonds in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Mn</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>7</mn></msub></math> is
question: The total number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Mn</mi><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math> bonds in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Mn</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>7</mn></msub></math> is
solution: The manganese heptoxide solubility properties indicate that it is a nonpolar molecular species, which is confirmed by its structure. The molecules consist of a pair of tetrahedra that share a common vertex. The vertices are occupied by oxygen atoms and at the centres of the tetrahedra are the Mn centres. The connectivity is indicated by the formula O3Mn−O−MnO3.

subject: Chemistry
q: In the reaction of potassium dichromate, potassium chloride and sulfuric acid (conc.), the oxidation state of the chromium in the product is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mo>+</mo></mfenced></math> _______.
question: In the reaction of potassium dichromate, potassium chloride and sulfuric acid (conc.), the oxidation state of the chromium in the product is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mo>+</mo></mfenced></math> _______.
solution:
The oxidation state or oxidation number is defined as the loss or gain of electrons from an atom, molecule, or compound.

The reaction of potassium dichromate, potassium chloride and sulphuric acid (conc.)is as follows,

K2Cr2O7s+4KCls+6H2SO4 (conc.)

→2CrO2Cl2g+6KHSO4+3H2O

This reaction takes place in chromyl chloride test.

The Chromyl chloride is a neutral molecule as it does not have any charge on it.

Here, oxidation state of Cr  in CrO2Cl2 is +6.

q: The major components in "Gun Metal" are:
question: The major components in "Gun Metal" are:
solution:
Gun metal is used for making guns and is also known as red brass. Gun metals are resistant to corrosion that is caused by steam and salt water. That’s why their composition includes metals which provides resistance to corrosion. Usually, gun metal is a type of bronze which is an alloy of copper, tin and zinc.

The major components in "Gun Metal" are

Cu:87%

Zn:3%

Sn:10%

q:
Arrange the following elements in the increasing order of number of unpaired electrons in it.
(A) \(\mathrm{Sc}\)
(B) \(\mathrm{Cr}\)
(C) \(\mathrm{V}\)
(D) \(\mathrm{Ti}\)
(E) Mn
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Arrange the following elements in the increasing order of number of unpaired electrons in it.
(A) \(\mathrm{Sc}\)
(B) \(\mathrm{Cr}\)
(C) \(\mathrm{V}\)
(D) \(\mathrm{Ti}\)
(E) Mn
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
$\begin{array}{ll} 
\text{Unpaired electron} \\
\operatorname{Sc}[\mathrm{Ar}] 4 \mathrm{~s}^2 3 \mathrm{~d}^1 & 1 \\
\mathrm{Cr}[\mathrm{Ar}] 4 \mathrm{~s}^1 3 \mathrm{~d}^5 & 6 \\
\mathrm{V}[\mathrm{Ar}] 4 \mathrm{~s}^2 3 \mathrm{~d}^3 & 3 \\

\end{array}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Choose the correct statements from the following
A. Mn2O7 is an oil at room temperature  B. V2O4 reacts with acid to give VO22+
C. CrO is a basic oxide  D. V2O5 does not react with acid
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Choose the correct statements from the following
A. Mn2O7 is an oil at room temperature  B. V2O4 reacts with acid to give VO22+
C. CrO is a basic oxide  D. V2O5 does not react with acid
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
All the metals except scandium form MO oxides, which are ionic. Mn2O7 is a covalent green oil.  In vanadium there is gradual change from the basic V2O3 to less basic V2O4 and to amphoteric V2O5 . V2O4 dissolves in acids to give VO2+ salts. Similarly, V2O5 reacts with alkalies as well as acids to give VO43- and VO4+ respectively. The well characterised CrO is basic but Cr2O3 is amphoteric.

(A) Mn2O7 is green oil at room temperature.

(B) V2O4 dissolve in acids to give VO2+ salts.

(C) CrO is basic oxide.

Hence option B is the answer,

subject: Chemistry
q:
The correct order of first $(\Delta_i H_1)$ and second $(\Delta_i H_2)$ ionisation enthalpy values of Cr and Mn are :
A. $\Delta_i H_1 :$ Cr $>$ Mn
B. $\Delta_i H_2 :$ Cr $>$ Mn
C. $\Delta_i H_1 :$ Mn $>$ Cr
D. $\Delta_i H_2 :$ Mn $>$ Cr
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
The correct order of first $(\Delta_i H_1)$ and second $(\Delta_i H_2)$ ionisation enthalpy values of Cr and Mn are :
A. $\Delta_i H_1 :$ Cr $>$ Mn
B. $\Delta_i H_2 :$ Cr $>$ Mn
C. $\Delta_i H_1 :$ Mn $>$ Cr
D. $\Delta_i H_2 :$ Mn $>$ Cr
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
The electronic configuration of Cr is $[Ar] 3d^5 4s^1$ and Mn is $[Ar] 3d^5 4s^2$.

For the first ionization enthalpy, the electron is removed from the $4s$ orbital. Mn has a fully filled $4s^2$ configuration and a higher effective nuclear charge compared to Cr. Thus, the first ionization enthalpy of Mn is greater than that of Cr ($\Delta_i H_1 :$ Mn $>$ Cr). This makes statement C correct.

After the first ionization, the electronic configurations become:
$Cr^+ : [Ar] 3d^5$
$Mn^+ : [Ar] 3d^5 4s^1$

For the second ionization enthalpy, the electron is removed from the highly stable half-filled $3d^5$ subshell in $Cr^+$, whereas in $Mn^+$, it is removed from the $4s^1$ orbital. Removing an electron from a stable half-filled configuration requires significantly more energy. Thus, the second ionization enthalpy of Cr is greater than that of Mn ($\Delta_i H_2 :$ Cr $>$ Mn). This makes statement B correct.

Therefore, the correct statements are B and C.

Answer: B and C only

subject: Chemistry
q: The spin only magnetic moment ( $\mu$ ) value (B.M.) of the compound with strongest oxidising power among $\mathrm{Mn}_2 \mathrm{O}_3, \mathrm{TiO}$ and VO is ____ B.M. (Nearest integer).
question: The spin only magnetic moment ( $\mu$ ) value (B.M.) of the compound with strongest oxidising power among $\mathrm{Mn}_2 \mathrm{O}_3, \mathrm{TiO}$ and VO is ____ B.M. (Nearest integer).
solution:
Strongest oxidising power among the option is $\mathrm{Mn}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ because of $\mathrm{E}^{\circ}$ value
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Mn}^{+3} / \mathrm{Mn}^{+2}}^{\circ}=+1.57 \mathrm{~V} \\
& \mathrm{Mn}^{+3} \rightarrow \mathrm{~d}^4 \text { configuration } \\
& \mu=\sqrt{24} \mathrm{BM} \\
& =4.89 \mathrm{BM} \\
& \Rightarrow 5
\end{aligned}$

decrease in atomic radii and increase in ionic radii.

decrease in both atomic and ionic radii.

solution:

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following statements about manganate and permanganate ions. Identify the correct statements.
A. The geometry of both manganate and permanganate ions is tetrahedral.
B. The oxidation states of Mn in manganate and permanganate are +7 and +6, respectively.
C. Oxidation of Mn (II) salt by peroxodisulphate gives manganate ion as the final product.
D. Manganate ion is paramagnetic and permanganate ions is diamagnetic.
E. Acidified permanganate ion reduces oxalate, nitrite and iodide ions.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Consider the following statements about manganate and permanganate ions. Identify the correct statements.
A. The geometry of both manganate and permanganate ions is tetrahedral.
B. The oxidation states of Mn in manganate and permanganate are +7 and +6, respectively.
C. Oxidation of Mn (II) salt by peroxodisulphate gives manganate ion as the final product.
D. Manganate ion is paramagnetic and permanganate ions is diamagnetic.
E. Acidified permanganate ion reduces oxalate, nitrite and iodide ions.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Manganate ion → $\text{MnO}_4^{2-}$
Permanganate ion → $\text{MnO}_4^{-}$
(A) Both are tetrahedral ($d^3s$ hybridisation) TRUE
(B) $\text{MnO}_4^{-}$ has Mn in +7 oxidation state; $\text{MnO}_4^{2-}$ has Mn in +6 oxidation state. The statement says +7 for manganate and +6 for permanganate, which is reversed. FALSE
(C) Oxidation of $\text{Mn}^{2+}$ by peroxodisulphate ($\text{S}_2\text{O}_8^{2-}$) gives permanganate ion ($\text{MnO}_4^{-}$), not manganate ion. FALSE
(D) $\text{MnO}_4^{-}$ is diamagnetic; $\text{MnO}_4^{2-}$ is paramagnetic. TRUE
(E) Acidified permanganate ion oxidises (not reduces) oxalate, nitrite and iodide ions. FALSE
Correct statements: A and D only.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following is NOT a physical or chemical characteristics of interstitial compounds?
question: Which of the following is NOT a physical or chemical characteristics of interstitial compounds?
options: They have high melting points, higher than those of pure metals.
They are very soft and ionic in nature.
They are chemically inert and usually non-stoichiometric.
solution:
Interstitial compounds are formed when small atoms like H, C, or N are trapped inside the crystal lattices of transition metals.

The principal physical and chemical characteristics of interstitial compounds are:
(i) They have high melting points, higher than those of pure metals.
(ii) They are very hard, and some borides approach diamond in hardness.
(iii) They retain metallic conductivity.
(iv) They are chemically inert.
(v) They are usually non-stoichiometric.

Therefore, the statement that they are very soft and ionic in nature is incorrect. They are very hard and are neither typically ionic nor covalent.

Answer: They are very soft and ionic in nature.

q: In neutral or alkaline solution, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mi>MnO</mi><mn>4</mn><mo>-</mo></msubsup></math> oxidises thiosulphate to
question: In neutral or alkaline solution, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mi>MnO</mi><mn>4</mn><mo>-</mo></msubsup></math> oxidises thiosulphate to
solution:
KMnO4 is a strong oxidising agent in acidic,alkaline or neutral medium as well

Thiosulphate is oxidised almost quantitatively to sulphate:

8MnO4+-3S2O3+2-H2O⟶8MnO2+6SO4+2-2OH-

The equivalent weight of KMnO4 in the above reaction =1585

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following acts as a strong reducing agent? (Atomic number : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Ce</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>58</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>Eu</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>63</mn></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Gd</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>64</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>Lu</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>71</mn><mo>)</mo></math>
question: Which of the following acts as a strong reducing agent? (Atomic number : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Ce</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>58</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>Eu</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>63</mn></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Gd</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>64</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>Lu</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>71</mn><mo>)</mo></math>
solution:
A reducing agent is one of the reactants of an oxidation-reduction reaction which reduces the other reactant by giving out electrons to the reactant. If the reducing agent does not pass electrons to other substances in a reaction, then the reduction process cannot occur.

Europium (atomic number 63) has the electronic configuration Xe4f76s2. It loses two electrons from the 6s energy level and attains a highly stable, half-filled 4f7 configuration, and hence it readily forms Eu2+ion. Eu2+ then changes to the common oxidation states of lanthanides +3 and forms Eu3+, acting as a strong reducing agent.

Eu+2→Eu+3+1e-[Xe]4f76 s0[Xe]4f66 s0
